[Monoamine oxidase](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase "Monoamine oxidase") enzymes: [MAO-A](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_A "Monoamine oxidase A") and/or [MAO-B](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_B "Monoamine oxidase B")

(**MAOIs**) are a class of drug that inhibit the activity of one or both [monoamine oxidase](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase "Monoamine oxidase") [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme"): [monoamine oxidase A](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_A "Monoamine oxidase A") (MAO-A) and [monoamine oxidase B](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_B "Monoamine oxidase B") (MAO-B). MAOIs are effective [antidepressants](/wiki/Antidepressant "Antidepressant") due to their specialized function of the inhibition of the enzyme that is responsible for neurotransmitter degradation in the synaptic cleft. This is especially true for [treatment-resistant depression](/wiki/Treatment-resistant_depression "Treatment-resistant depression"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) which is a type of depression that is resistant to common treatments of typical depression, such as selective-serotonin reuptake inhibitors ([SSRIs](/wiki/Selective_serotonin_reuptake_inhibitor "Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor")) or serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors ([SNRIs](/wiki/Serotonin%E2%80%93norepinephrine_reuptake_inhibitor "Serotonin–norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor")).

**Monoamine oxidase inhibitors** (**MAOIs**) are a class of drug that inhibit the activity of one or both [monoamine oxidase](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase "Monoamine oxidase") [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme"): [monoamine oxidase A](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_A "Monoamine oxidase A") (MAO-A) and [monoamine oxidase B](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_B "Monoamine oxidase B") (MAO-B). MAOIs are effective [antidepressants](/wiki/Antidepressant "Antidepressant") due to their specialized function of the inhibition of the enzyme that is responsible for neurotransmitter degradation in the synaptic cleft. This is especially true for [treatment-resistant depression](/wiki/Treatment-resistant_depression "Treatment-resistant depression"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) which is a type of depression that is resistant to common treatments of typical depression, such as selective-serotonin reuptake inhibitors ([SSRIs](/wiki/Selective_serotonin_reuptake_inhibitor "Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor")) or serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors ([SNRIs](/wiki/Serotonin%E2%80%93norepinephrine_reuptake_inhibitor "Serotonin–norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor")).

MAOIs are also utilized to treat [panic disorder](/wiki/Panic_disorder "Panic disorder"), [social anxiety disorder](/wiki/Social_anxiety_disorder "Social anxiety disorder"), [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"), and several other disorders.

Reversible inhibitors of monoamine oxidase A (RIMAs) are a subclass of MAOIs that [selectively](/wiki/Binding_selectivity "Binding selectivity") and [reversibly](/wiki/Enzyme_inhibitor#Reversible_inhibitors "Enzyme inhibitor") [inhibit](/wiki/Enzyme_inhibitor "Enzyme inhibitor") the MAO-A enzyme. RIMAs are used clinically in the [treatment](/wiki/Medication "Medication") of [depression](/wiki/Major_depressive_disorder "Major depressive disorder") and [dysthymia](/wiki/Dysthymia "Dysthymia"). Due to their reversibility, they are safer in single-drug overdose than the older, irreversible MAOIs,[[2]](#cite_note-2) and weaker in increasing the monoamines important in depressive disorder.[[3]](#cite_note-3) RIMAs have not gained widespread market share in the United States.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Moclobemide_failure.png/250px-Moclobemide_failure.png)](/wiki/File:Moclobemide_failure.png)

How RIMAs work and why RIMAs can only minimally increase depression-related neurotransmitters

## Medical uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Medical uses")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Moclobemide.svg/250px-Moclobemide.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Moclobemide.svg)

[Skeletal formula](/wiki/Skeletal_formula "Skeletal formula") of [moclobemide](/wiki/Moclobemide "Moclobemide"), the prototypical RIMA.

MAOIs have been found to be effective in the treatment of [panic disorder](/wiki/Panic_disorder "Panic disorder") with [agoraphobia](/wiki/Agoraphobia "Agoraphobia"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) [social phobia](/wiki/Social_phobia "Social phobia"),[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) atypical depression[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) or mixed [anxiety disorder](/wiki/Anxiety_disorder "Anxiety disorder") and depression, [bulimia](/wiki/Bulimia "Bulimia"),[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) and [post-traumatic stress disorder](/wiki/Post-traumatic_stress_disorder "Post-traumatic stress disorder"),[[14]](#cite_note-14) as well as [borderline personality disorder](/wiki/Borderline_personality_disorder "Borderline personality disorder"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) and [obsessive–compulsive disorder](/wiki/Obsessive%E2%80%93compulsive_disorder "Obsessive–compulsive disorder") (OCD).[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) MAOIs appear to be particularly effective in the management of [bipolar depression](/wiki/Bipolar_disorder "Bipolar disorder") according to a retrospective-analysis from 2009.[[18]](#cite_note-18) There are reports of MAOI efficacy in OCD, [trichotillomania](/wiki/Trichotillomania "Trichotillomania"), [body dysmorphic disorder](/wiki/Body_dysmorphic_disorder "Body dysmorphic disorder"), and [avoidant personality disorder](/wiki/Avoidant_personality_disorder "Avoidant personality disorder"), but these reports are from uncontrolled case reports.[[19]](#cite_note-pmid2248064-19)

MAOIs can also be used in the treatment of Parkinson's disease by targeting MAO-B in particular (therefore affecting [dopaminergic neurons](/wiki/Dopaminergic_neuron "Dopaminergic neuron")), as well as providing an alternative for [migraine](/wiki/Migraine "Migraine") [prophylaxis](/wiki/Prophylaxis "Prophylaxis"). Inhibition of both MAO-A and MAO-B is used in the treatment of [clinical depression](/wiki/Clinical_depression "Clinical depression") and [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety").

MAOIs appear to be particularly indicated for [outpatients](/wiki/Outpatient "Outpatient") with [dysthymia](/wiki/Dysthymia "Dysthymia") complicated by panic disorder or [hysteroid dysphoria](/wiki/Hysteroid_dysphoria "Hysteroid dysphoria").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Newer MAOIs such as [selegiline](/wiki/Selegiline "Selegiline") (typically used in the treatment of Parkinson's disease) and the reversible MAOI [moclobemide](/wiki/Moclobemide "Moclobemide") provide a safer alternative[[19]](#cite_note-pmid2248064-19) and are now sometimes used as first-line therapy.

[Pargyline](/wiki/Pargyline "Pargyline") is a non-selective MAOI that was previously used as an [antihypertensive agent](/wiki/Antihypertensive_agent "Antihypertensive agent") to treat [hypertension](/wiki/Hypertension "Hypertension") (high blood pressure).[[21]](#cite_note-Finberg2014-21)[[22]](#cite_note-HayesSchneck1976-22)

## Side effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Side effects")]

### Hypertensive crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Hypertensive crisis")]

People taking MAOIs generally need to change their diets to limit or avoid foods and beverages containing [tyramine](/wiki/Tyramine "Tyramine").[[23]](#cite_note-23) If large amounts of tyramine are consumed, they may develop a [hypertensive crisis](/wiki/Hypertensive_crisis "Hypertensive crisis"), which can be fatal.[[24]](#cite_note-Stahl-24) Examples of [foods and beverages with potentially high levels of tyramine](/wiki/Tyramine#Occurrence "Tyramine") include cheese, [Chianti wine](/wiki/Chianti_wine "Chianti wine"), and pickled fish.[[25]](#cite_note-Mosher-25) Excessive concentrations of tyramine in blood plasma can lead to hypertensive crisis by increasing the release of [norepinephrine (NE)](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine"), which causes blood vessels to constrict by activating [alpha-1 adrenergic receptors](/wiki/Alpha-1_adrenergic_receptor "Alpha-1 adrenergic receptor").[[26]](#cite_note-:0-26) Ordinarily, MAO-A would destroy the excess NE; when MAO-A is inhibited, however, NE levels get too high, leading to dangerous increases in blood pressure.

RIMAs are displaced from MAO-A in the presence of [tyramine](/wiki/Tyramine "Tyramine"),[[27]](#cite_note-Lotufo-Neto-27) rather than inhibiting its breakdown in the liver as general MAOIs do. Additionally, [MAO-B](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_B "Monoamine oxidase B") remains free and continues to metabolize tyramine in the stomach, although this is less significant than the liver action. Thus, RIMAs are unlikely to elicit tyramine-mediated hypertensive crisis; moreover, dietary modifications are not usually necessary when taking a reversible inhibitor of MAO-A (i.e., [moclobemide](/wiki/Moclobemide "Moclobemide")) or low doses of selective MAO-B inhibitors (e.g., [selegiline 6 mg/24 hours transdermal patch](/wiki/Emsam "Emsam")).[[26]](#cite_note-:0-26)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Drug interactions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Drug interactions")]

The most significant risk associated with the use of MAOIs is the potential for [drug interactions](/wiki/Drug_interaction "Drug interaction") with over-the-counter, prescription, or illegally obtained medications, and some [dietary supplements](/wiki/Dietary_supplement "Dietary supplement") (e.g., [St. John's wort](/wiki/St._John%27s_wort "St. John's wort") or [tryptophan](/wiki/Tryptophan "Tryptophan")). It is vital that a doctor supervise such combinations to avoid adverse reactions. For this reason, many users carry an MAOI-card, which lets emergency medical personnel know what drugs to avoid (e.g. adrenaline [epinephrine] dosage should be reduced by 75%, and duration is extended).[[25]](#cite_note-Mosher-25)

Tryptophan supplements can be consumed with MAOIs, but can result in transient [serotonin syndrome](/wiki/Serotonin_syndrome "Serotonin syndrome").[[30]](#cite_note-Boyer-NEJM-30)

MAOIs should not be combined with other psychoactive substances (antidepressants, painkillers, stimulants, including prescribed, OTC and illegally acquired drugs, etc.) except under expert care. Certain combinations can cause lethal reactions; common examples include [SSRIs](/wiki/Selective_serotonin_reuptake_inhibitor "Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor"), [tricyclics](/wiki/Tricyclic_antidepressant "Tricyclic antidepressant"), [MDMA](/wiki/MDMA "MDMA"), [meperidine](/wiki/Meperidine "Meperidine"),[[31]](#cite_note-31) [tramadol](/wiki/Tramadol "Tramadol"), and [dextromethorphan](/wiki/Dextromethorphan "Dextromethorphan"),[[32]](#cite_note-32) whereas combinations with certain [psychedelics](/wiki/Psychedelic_drug "Psychedelic drug") like [LSD](/wiki/LSD "LSD") or [psilocybin](/wiki/Psilocybin "Psilocybin") appear to be relatively safe.[[33]](#cite_note-RachedCampanaFiani2026-33)[[34]](#cite_note-MalcolmThomas2022-34) On the other hand, MAOIs can strongly potentiate psychedelics metabolized by MAO like [dimethyltryptamine](/wiki/Dimethyltryptamine "Dimethyltryptamine") (DMT), [5-MeO-DMT](/wiki/5-MeO-DMT "5-MeO-DMT"), and [2C-B](/wiki/2C-B "2C-B"), potentially resulting in serious [toxicity](/wiki/Toxicity "Toxicity") or death.[[33]](#cite_note-RachedCampanaFiani2026-33) Findings for [mescaline](/wiki/Mescaline "Mescaline") are less clear.[[33]](#cite_note-RachedCampanaFiani2026-33) Drugs that affect the release or reuptake of epinephrine, norepinephrine, serotonin or dopamine typically need to be administered at lower doses due to the resulting potentiated and prolonged effect. MAOIs also interact with [tobacco](/wiki/Tobacco "Tobacco")-containing products (e.g. cigarettes) and may potentiate the effects of certain compounds in tobacco.[[35]](#cite_note-pmid11343627-35)[[36]](#cite_note-pmid8943061-36)[[37]](#cite_note-FowlerVolkow1996-37) This may be reflected in the difficulty of smoking cessation, as tobacco contains naturally occurring MAOI compounds in addition to the [nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine "Nicotine").[[35]](#cite_note-pmid11343627-35)[[36]](#cite_note-pmid8943061-36)[[37]](#cite_note-FowlerVolkow1996-37)

While safer than general MAOIs, RIMAs still possess significant and potentially serious drug interactions with many common drugs; in particular, they can cause [serotonin syndrome](/wiki/Serotonin_syndrome "Serotonin syndrome") or hypertensive crisis when combined with almost any [antidepressant](/wiki/Antidepressant "Antidepressant") or [stimulant](/wiki/Stimulant "Stimulant"), common migraine medications, certain herbs, or most cold medicines (including [decongestants](/wiki/Decongestant "Decongestant"), [antihistamines](/wiki/Antihistamine "Antihistamine"), and [cough syrup](/wiki/Cough_syrup "Cough syrup")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Ocular [alpha-2 agonists](/wiki/Alpha-2_agonist "Alpha-2 agonist") such as [brimonidine](/wiki/Brimonidine "Brimonidine") and [apraclonidine](/wiki/Apraclonidine "Apraclonidine") are glaucoma medications which reduce intraocular pressure by decreasing aqueous production. These alpha-2 agonists should not be given with oral MAOIs due to the risk of hypertensive crisis.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Withdrawal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Withdrawal")]

Main article: [Antidepressant discontinuation syndrome](/wiki/Antidepressant_discontinuation_syndrome "Antidepressant discontinuation syndrome")

See also: [Tapering (medicine)](/wiki/Tapering_(medicine) "Tapering (medicine)")

[Antidepressants](/wiki/Antidepressants "Antidepressants") including MAOIs have some [dependence](/wiki/Substance_dependence "Substance dependence")-producing effects, the most notable one being a [discontinuation syndrome](/wiki/Antidepressant_discontinuation_syndrome "Antidepressant discontinuation syndrome"), which may be severe especially if MAOIs are discontinued abruptly or too rapidly. The dependence-producing potential of MAOIs or antidepressants in general is not as significant as [benzodiazepines](/wiki/Benzodiazepines "Benzodiazepines"). Discontinuation symptoms can be managed by [tapering](/wiki/Tapering_(medicine) "Tapering (medicine)"), a gradual reduction in dosage over a period of days, weeks or sometimes months to minimize or prevent withdrawal symptoms.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

MAOIs, as with most antidepressant medication, may not alter the course of the disorder in a significant, permanent way, so it is possible that discontinuation can return the patient to the pre-treatment state.[[40]](#cite_note-40) This consideration complicates prescribing between an MAOI and an SSRI, because it is necessary to clear the system completely of one drug before starting another. One physician organization recommends the dose to be tapered down over a minimum of four weeks, followed by a two-week washout period.[[41]](#cite_note-ranz-41) The result is that a depressed patient will have to bear the depression without chemical help during the drug-free interval. This may be preferable to risking the effects of an interaction between the two drugs.[[41]](#cite_note-ranz-41)

## Mechanism of action

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Mechanism of action")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Monoamine_oxidase_A_2BXS.png/250px-Monoamine_oxidase_A_2BXS.png)](/wiki/File:Monoamine_oxidase_A_2BXS.png)

[Ribbon diagram](/wiki/Ribbon_diagram "Ribbon diagram") of a [monomer](/wiki/Monomer "Monomer") of human MAO-A, with [FAD](/wiki/Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide "Flavin adenine dinucleotide") and [clorgiline](/wiki/Clorgiline "Clorgiline") bound, oriented as if attached to the [outer membrane](/wiki/Outer_mitochondrial_membrane "Outer mitochondrial membrane") of a [mitochondrion](/wiki/Mitochondrion "Mitochondrion"). From [PDB](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank "Protein Data Bank"): [2BXS](https://www.rcsb.org/structure/2BXS)​.

MAOIs act by inhibiting the activity of [monoamine oxidase](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase "Monoamine oxidase"). Monoamine oxidase is an enzyme that is responsible for the degradation of [monoamine neurotransmitters](/wiki/Monoamine_neurotransmitter "Monoamine neurotransmitter"), such as dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine, and prevents these neurotransmitters from remaining in the synaptic cleft for extended periods of time. Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitors are responsible for inhibiting monoamine oxidase, ultimately leading to prevention of the degradation of neurotransmitters in the synaptic cleft, leading to higher concentrations of neurotransmitters remaining in the synaptic cleft for longer periods of time.

There are two [isoforms](/wiki/Isoform "Isoform") of monoamine oxidase, MAO-A and MAO-B. MAO-A preferentially [deaminates](/wiki/Deamination "Deamination") [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin"), [melatonin](/wiki/Melatonin "Melatonin"), [epinephrine](/wiki/Epinephrine "Epinephrine"), and [norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine"). MAO-B preferentially deaminates [phenethylamine](/wiki/Phenethylamine "Phenethylamine") and certain other [trace amines](/wiki/Trace_amine "Trace amine"); in contrast, MAO-A preferentially deaminates other trace amines, like [tyramine](/wiki/Tyramine "Tyramine"), whereas [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") is equally deaminated by both types.

### Reversibility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Reversibility")]

The early MAOIs covalently bound to the monoamine oxidase enzymes, thus inhibiting them irreversibly; the bound enzyme could not function and thus enzyme activity was blocked until the cell made new enzymes. The enzymes turn over approximately every two weeks. A few newer MAOIs, a notable one being [moclobemide](/wiki/Moclobemide "Moclobemide"), are reversible, meaning that they are able to detach from the enzyme to facilitate usual [catabolism](/wiki/Catabolism "Catabolism") of the [substrate](/wiki/Substrate_(biochemistry) "Substrate (biochemistry)"). The level of inhibition in this way is governed by the concentrations of the substrate and the MAOI.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[Harmaline](/wiki/Harmaline "Harmaline") found in *[Peganum harmala](/wiki/Peganum_harmala "Peganum harmala")*, *[Banisteriopsis caapi](/wiki/Banisteriopsis_caapi "Banisteriopsis caapi")*, and *[Passiflora incarnata](/wiki/Passiflora_incarnata "Passiflora incarnata")* is a reversible inhibitor of monoamine oxidase A (RIMA).[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Selectivity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Selectivity")]

In addition to reversibility, MAOIs differ by their selectivity of the MAO enzyme subtype. Some MAOIs inhibit both MAO-A and MAO-B equally, other MAOIs have been developed to target one over the other.

MAO-A inhibition reduces the breakdown of primarily serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine; selective inhibition of MAO-A allows for [tyramine](/wiki/Tyramine "Tyramine") to be metabolised via MAO-B.[[44]](#cite_note-pmid9432289-44) Agents that act on serotonin, if taken with another serotonin-enhancing agent, may result in a potentially fatal interaction called [serotonin syndrome](/wiki/Serotonin_syndrome "Serotonin syndrome"); if taken with irreversible and unselective inhibitors (such as older MAOIs) a [hypertensive crisis](/wiki/Hypertensive_crisis "Hypertensive crisis") may result due to tyramine food interactions. Tyramine is broken down by MAO-A and MAO-B, therefore inhibiting this action may result in its excessive build-up, so diet must be monitored for tyramine intake.

MAO-B inhibition reduces the breakdown mainly of dopamine and [phenethylamine](/wiki/Phenethylamine "Phenethylamine"), so there are no associated dietary restrictions. MAO-B would also metabolize tyramine, as the only differences between dopamine, phenethylamine, and tyramine are two phenylhydroxyl groups on carbons 3 and 4. The 4-OH would not be a steric hindrance to MAO-B on tyramine.[[45]](#cite_note-45) [Selegiline](/wiki/Selegiline "Selegiline") is selective for MAO-B at low doses, but non-selective at higher doses.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: History")]

The knowledge of MAOIs began with the serendipitous discovery that [iproniazid](/wiki/Iproniazid "Iproniazid") was a potent MAO inhibitor (MAOI).[[46]](#cite_note-MAOIstoSSRIs-46) Originally intended for the treatment of tuberculosis, in 1952, iproniazid's antidepressant properties were discovered when researchers noted that the depressed patients given iproniazid experienced a relief of their depression. Subsequent in vitro work led to the discovery that it inhibited MAO and eventually to the [monoamine theory of depression](/wiki/Biology_of_depression "Biology of depression"). MAOIs became widely used as antidepressants in the early 1950s. The discovery of the 2 isoenzymes of MAO has led to the development of selective MAOIs that may have a more favorable side-effect profile.[[47]](#cite_note-CNSDrugs2013-47)

The older MAOIs' heyday was mostly between the years 1957 and 1970.[[44]](#cite_note-pmid9432289-44) The initial popularity of the 'classic' non-selective irreversible MAO inhibitors began to wane due to their serious interactions with [sympathomimetic](/wiki/Sympathomimetic "Sympathomimetic") drugs and [tyramine](/wiki/Tyramine "Tyramine")-containing foods that could lead to dangerous hypertensive emergencies. As a result, the use by medical practitioners of these older MAOIs declined. When scientists discovered that there are two different MAO enzymes (MAO-A and MAO-B), they developed selective compounds for MAO-B, (for example, [selegiline](/wiki/Selegiline "Selegiline"), which is used for Parkinson's disease), to reduce the side-effects and serious interactions. Further improvement occurred with the development of compounds ([moclobemide](/wiki/Moclobemide "Moclobemide") and [toloxatone](/wiki/Toloxatone "Toloxatone")) that not only are selective but cause reversible MAO-A inhibition and a reduction in dietary and drug interactions.[[48]](#cite_note-pmid8713690-48)[[49]](#cite_note-pmid7905288-49) [Moclobemide](/wiki/Moclobemide "Moclobemide"), was the first reversible inhibitor of MAO-A to enter widespread clinical practice.[[50]](#cite_note-pmid8490690-50)

A transdermal patch form of the MAOI [selegiline](/wiki/Selegiline "Selegiline"), called [Emsam](/wiki/Emsam "Emsam"), was approved for use in depression by the [Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") on 28 February 2006.[[51]](#cite_note-Emsam-51)
